title: Figure-Eight with Simple Changes at the Sitting Trot
steps: Set up a figure-eight using two 20-meter circles meeting at X.
Begin at the sitting trot on the right lead on the right circle.
As you approach X, transition to sitting sitting trot for 3-5 steps.
Pick up the left lead and ride the left circle at the sitting trot.
After one full circle, return to X, sitting sitting trot for 3-5 steps, and pick up the right lead again.
Repeat 4-6 times. The sitting sitting trot steps should be clean and forward, not shuffling.
This is the fundamental simple change exercise used in Second Level dressage tests.
mistakes: Trotting instead of walking between lead changes.
Too many walk steps, losing the connection between canters.
Picking up the wrong lead after the walk transition.
The figure-eight becoming lopsided with one circle larger than the other.
variations: Reduce walk steps to 2-3 for a more advanced version.
Progress toward flying changes by eventually eliminating the walk steps entirely.
Use 15-meter circles instead of 20 for increased collection demand.
tips: Tip: Think of the walk steps as a brief rest stop, not a destination. The canter energy should stay alive through the walk.

title: Figure-Eight with Simple Changes at the Collected Trot
steps: Set up a figure-eight using two 20-meter circles meeting at X.
Begin at the collected trot on the right lead on the right circle.
As you approach X, transition to collected collected trot for 3-5 steps.
Pick up the left lead and ride the left circle at the collected trot.
After one full circle, return to X, collected collected trot for 3-5 steps, and pick up the right lead again.
Repeat 4-6 times. The collected collected trot steps should be clean and forward, not shuffling.
This is the fundamental simple change exercise used in Second Level dressage tests.
mistakes: Trotting instead of walking between lead changes.

title: Half-Pass Zigzag at Walk
steps: Start on the center line at the collected walk. Half-pass to the right, moving diagonally toward the right track.
After reaching the quarter line, straighten for 2-3 strides, then change the bend and half-pass to the left.
Continue the zigzag: half-pass right to the quarter line, straighten, half-pass left to the opposite quarter line.
Each half-pass segment should cover equal distance both forward and sideways.
The changes of bend should happen in the 2-3 straight strides. The horse must be supple enough to switch quickly.
Complete 4 direction changes (2 zigzags), then ride forward on the center line and walk.
mistakes: The segments being uneven — more sideways in one direction than the other.
The horse losing collection during the direction changes.
Not enough straight strides between half-pass directions, causing a choppy transition.
The haunches leading instead of the shoulders in the half-pass portions.
variations: Progress to half-pass zigzag at the collected canter.
Increase to 6 direction changes for a longer exercise.
Vary the width of each zigzag segment for adjustability training.
tips: Tip: The half-pass zigzag is one of the most demanding dressage movements. Master half-pass in isolation before attempting zigzags.

title: Center Line Riding at the Walk
steps: At A, turn onto the center line heading toward C. Look straight ahead at C to guide your line.
Your horse should be absolutely straight on the center line. Use both legs evenly to prevent drift.
Check your straightness by glancing down at the line in the footing (if visible) or at the arena walls to ensure equal distance on both sides.
Ride the full center line at the walk. At C, turn left or right onto the track.
Repeat at the walk. Straightness becomes harder at faster gaits because any imbalance is magnified.
Practice the center line 4-6 times. This is a fundamental skill for every dressage test entry.
mistakes: Drifting to one side because the horse favors one shoulder over the other.
Looking down at the horse's neck instead of ahead at C.
The initial turn onto the center line being too shallow or too deep.
Losing straightness in the last 3-4 strides before C because the horse anticipates the turn.
variations: Add a halt at X for a halt-salute test preparation exercise.
Ride the center line in medium trot for added difficulty.
Practice the center line at the canter with a specific lead for test preparation.
tips: Tip: The center line reveals the truth about your horse's straightness. Film it from C and you'll see every drift and wobble.

title: 15-Meter Circle at the Walk
steps: Place four cones marking the quarter points of a 15-meter circle at E or B.
Pick up a working walk and begin riding the circle, aiming to pass directly over each cone.
Keep your inside leg at the girth to maintain bend. Your outside leg sits just behind the girth to prevent the haunches from swinging out.
Use your outside rein as a supporting rein to control the size of the circle. Avoid over-bending through the neck.
Ride 5 complete circles, then transition to walk and check your horse for straightness on a straight line.
Change direction and repeat on the other rein. Note which direction feels easier for your horse.
Progress by riding the circle without cones, then checking your track in the footing.
mistakes: Making an egg shape instead of a true circle because the horse drifts toward the gate.
Over-bending the neck while the shoulder falls out, creating a bulging circle.
Sitting crooked with more weight in the outside stirrup.
Rushing the tempo as the circle size decreases from 20 to 15 meters.
variations: Shrink to a 12-meter circle for 2 revolutions, then leg yield back out to 15 meters.
Add halt transitions on the circle, maintaining the curved track.
Alternate between 15-meter and 20-meter circles at the same arena letter.
tips: Tip: Think of your circle as a clock face. At each quarter point, briefly check: is my horse's body uniformly bent from poll to tail?

title: Medium Walk Across the Diagonal
steps: Establish a balanced working walk. Prepare for the diagonal line.
At the corner letter, turn across the diagonal. In the first 3-4 strides, ask the horse to lengthen into medium walk.
Allow the stride to grow longer and more ground-covering. The tempo stays the same — only the stride length increases.
Your posting should become bigger and more forward to match the longer stride.
Maintain the medium walk across the diagonal until 3-4 strides before the track.
Collect back to working walk before reaching the track. The collection should be smooth, not abrupt.
Change direction and repeat on the other diagonal.
mistakes: The horse running faster instead of lengthening the stride.
Losing balance and falling onto the forehand during the medium trot.
Not collecting soon enough and arriving at the track unbalanced.
The rider gripping with the knees and blocking the horse's shoulder movement.
variations: Perform medium trot on the long side for a different spatial challenge.
Add a 10-meter circle at X in the working trot before continuing medium trot.
Progress to extended trot across the diagonal for even greater lengthening.
tips: Tip: Medium trot should feel like the horse is pushing more from behind, not pulling from the front. The engine is in the hindquarters.

title: Medium Walk on the Long Side
steps: Establish a balanced working walk on the track. Sit deep and maintain a steady rhythm.
At the beginning of the long side, allow the walk to open up into medium walk.
Your hips follow the bigger walk stride. Soften your hands forward slightly to allow the horse to lengthen the frame.
The medium walk should cover more ground per stride without speeding up the tempo.
Maintain medium walk for the length of the long side.
Before the corner, collect back to working walk using half-halts from your core and outside rein.
The transition back to working walk should happen over 3-4 strides, not abruptly.
mistakes: The horse flattening and running instead of truly lengthening the canter stride.
Not collecting soon enough, causing a scrambled turn through the corner.
Losing the three-beat canter rhythm in the medium — it becomes four-beat.
Leaning back too much in the collection, pulling on the horse's mouth.
variations: Perform medium canter across the diagonal for a different spatial challenge.
Alternate medium and collected canter every half of the long side.
Progress to extended canter for maximum ground-coverage.
tips: Tip: Think of medium canter as turning up the volume on a speaker — same song, just bigger. The rhythm doesn't change.

steps: Establish a balanced working sitting trot on the track. Sit deep and maintain a steady rhythm.
At the beginning of the long side, allow the sitting trot to open up into medium sitting trot.
Your hips follow the bigger sitting trot stride. Soften your hands forward slightly to allow the horse to lengthen the frame.
The medium sitting trot should cover more ground per stride without speeding up the tempo.
Maintain medium sitting trot for the length of the long side.
Before the corner, collect back to working sitting trot using half-halts from your core and outside rein.
The transition back to working sitting trot should happen over 3-4 strides, not abruptly.
mistakes: The horse flattening and running instead of truly lengthening the canter stride.
Not collecting soon enough, causing a scrambled turn through the corner.
Losing the three-beat canter rhythm in the medium — it becomes four-beat.
Leaning back too much in the collection, pulling on the horse's mouth.
variations: Perform medium canter across the diagonal for a different spatial challenge.
Alternate medium and collected canter every half of the long side.
Progress to extended canter for maximum ground-coverage.
tips: Tip: Think of medium canter as turning up the volume on a speaker — same song, just bigger. The rhythm doesn't change.

title: Medium Collected Trot on the Long Side
steps: Establish a balanced working collected trot on the track. Sit deep and maintain a steady rhythm.
At the beginning of the long side, allow the collected trot to open up into medium collected trot.
Your hips follow the bigger collected trot stride. Soften your hands forward slightly to allow the horse to lengthen the frame.
The medium collected trot should cover more ground per stride without speeding up the tempo.
Maintain medium collected trot for the length of the long side.
Before the corner, collect back to working collected trot using half-halts from your core and outside rein.
The transition back to working collected trot should happen over 3-4 strides, not abruptly.
mistakes: The horse flattening and running instead of truly lengthening the canter stride.
Not collecting soon enough, causing a scrambled turn through the corner.
Losing the three-beat canter rhythm in the medium — it becomes four-beat.
Leaning back too much in the collection, pulling on the horse's mouth.
variations: Perform medium canter across the diagonal for a different spatial challenge.
Alternate medium and collected canter every half of the long side.
Progress to extended canter for maximum ground-coverage.
tips: Tip: Think of medium canter as turning up the volume on a speaker — same song, just bigger. The rhythm doesn't change.

title: Lengthening Strides at the Walk
steps: Set up 4 ground poles spaced 4.5 feet apart (normal walk stride distance).
Walk through the poles at a working walk. Feel the rhythm over the evenly-spaced poles.
Move the poles to 5 feet apart. Walk through again, allowing a slightly longer stride.
Move the poles to 5.5 feet apart. Now the horse must genuinely lengthen to reach each pole.
Focus on maintaining rhythm while covering more ground. The tempo stays the same — only the stride length changes.
Return to the 4.5-foot spacing and notice how the working walk feels more compact.
mistakes: The horse rushing and getting quick instead of lengthening the stride.
Tripping over poles because the horse is not paying attention to foot placement.
Losing balance after the poles because the lengthening threw the horse onto the forehand.
Spacing the poles too far apart, causing the horse to canter instead of trot.
variations: Use the same concept with canter poles (9-10 foot spacing, gradually increasing to 11-12 feet).
Ride the lengthening exercise without poles on the diagonal.
Add a collection exercise after the lengthening: lengthen down the long side, collect through the corner.
tips: Tip: Poles teach lengthening better than any other tool because the horse can see and feel the target stride length.

title: Lengthening Strides at the Trot at the Trot
steps: Set up 4 ground poles spaced 4.5 feet apart (normal trot stride distance).
Trot through the poles at a working trot. Feel the rhythm over the evenly-spaced poles.
Move the poles to 5 feet apart. Trot through again, allowing a slightly longer stride.
Move the poles to 5.5 feet apart. Now the horse must genuinely lengthen to reach each pole.
Focus on maintaining rhythm while covering more ground. The tempo stays the same — only the stride length changes.
Return to the 4.5-foot spacing and notice how the working trot feels more compact.
mistakes: The horse rushing and getting quick instead of lengthening the stride.
Tripping over poles because the horse is not paying attention to foot placement.
Losing balance after the poles because the lengthening threw the horse onto the forehand.
Spacing the poles too far apart, causing the horse to canter instead of trot.
variations: Use the same concept with canter poles (9-10 foot spacing, gradually increasing to 11-12 feet).
Ride the lengthening exercise without poles on the diagonal.
Add a collection exercise after the lengthening: lengthen down the long side, collect through the corner.
tips: Tip: Poles teach lengthening better than any other tool because the horse can see and feel the target stride length.

Move the poles to 5.5 feet apart. Now the horse must genuinely lengthen to reach each pole.

Return to the 4.5-foot spacing and notice how the working sitting trot feels more compact.

title: Lengthening Strides at the Collected Trot
steps: Set up 4 ground poles spaced 4.5 feet apart (normal collected trot stride distance).
Collected Trot through the poles at a working collected trot. Feel the rhythm over the evenly-spaced poles.
Move the poles to 5 feet apart. Collected Trot through again, allowing a slightly longer stride.
Move the poles to 5.5 feet apart. Now the horse must genuinely lengthen to reach each pole.
Focus on maintaining rhythm while covering more ground. The tempo stays the same — only the stride length changes.
Return to the 4.5-foot spacing and notice how the working collected trot feels more compact.
mistakes: The horse rushing and getting quick instead of lengthening the stride.

title: Walk-Trot Transitions on the Long Side at the Walk
steps: Track right at the working walk along the long side.
At the first letter (H, S, E, V, or K), ask for an upward transition to working walk.
Close your lower legs and allow your hips to follow the new rhythm. Keep your hands steady.
Walk to the next letter, then ask for a downward transition back to walk.
The downward transition should come from your seat and core: stop following the walk rhythm and close your fingers on the reins.
Alternate walk and walk at every letter along the long side.
Focus on making each transition happen exactly at the letter — not 3 strides early or late.
mistakes: The transition happening too late because the rider doesn't prepare early enough.
The trot departure being abrupt and fast instead of flowing forward smoothly.
The walk transition taking too many strides — the horse leaking forward instead of responding promptly.
The rider using too much rein for the downward transition instead of the seat and core.
variations: Add halt transitions between the walk-trot transitions for three-gait work.
Progress to trot-canter transitions at the letters.
Perform the transitions on a 20-meter circle instead of the long side.
tips: Tip: Prepare your transition 3 strides before the letter. Think of it as: prepare, ask, allow.

title: Trot-Trot Transitions on the Long Side
steps: Track right at the working trot along the long side.
At the first letter (H, S, E, V, or K), ask for an upward transition to working trot.
Close your lower legs and allow your hips to follow the new rhythm. Keep your hands steady.
Trot to the next letter, then ask for a downward transition back to trot.
The downward transition should come from your seat and core: stop following the trot rhythm and close your fingers on the reins.
Alternate trot and trot at every letter along the long side.
Focus on making each transition happen exactly at the letter — not 3 strides early or late.
mistakes: The transition happening too late because the rider doesn't prepare early enough.
The trot departure being abrupt and fast instead of flowing forward smoothly.
The walk transition taking too many strides — the horse leaking forward instead of responding promptly.
The rider using too much rein for the downward transition instead of the seat and core.
variations: Add halt transitions between the walk-trot transitions for three-gait work.
Progress to trot-canter transitions at the letters.
Perform the transitions on a 20-meter circle instead of the long side.
tips: Tip: Prepare your transition 3 strides before the letter. Think of it as: prepare, ask, allow.

title: Canter-Trot Transitions on the Long Side
steps: Track right at the working canter along the long side.
At the first letter (H, S, E, V, or K), ask for an upward transition to working canter.
Close your lower legs and allow your hips to follow the new rhythm. Keep your hands steady.
Canter to the next letter, then ask for a downward transition back to canter.
The downward transition should come from your seat and core: stop following the canter rhythm and close your fingers on the reins.
Alternate canter and canter at every letter along the long side.
Focus on making each transition happen exactly at the letter — not 3 strides early or late.
mistakes: The transition happening too late because the rider doesn't prepare early enough.

title: Sitting Trot-Trot Transitions on the Long Side

At the first letter (H, S, E, V, or K), ask for an upward transition to working sitting trot.
Close your lower legs and allow your hips to follow the new rhythm. Keep your hands steady.
Sitting Trot to the next letter, then ask for a downward transition back to sitting sitting trot.
The downward transition should come from your seat and core: stop following the sitting trot rhythm and close your fingers on the reins.
Alternate sitting sitting trot and sitting trot at every letter along the long side.
Focus on making each transition happen exactly at the letter — not 3 strides early or late.

Perform the transitions on a 20-meter circle instead of the long side.
tips: Tip: Prepare your transition 3 strides before the letter. Think of it as: prepare, ask, allow.

title: Collected Trot-Trot Transitions on the Long Side
steps: Track right at the working collected collected trot along the long side.
At the first letter (H, S, E, V, or K), ask for an upward transition to working collected trot.
Close your lower legs and allow your hips to follow the new rhythm. Keep your hands steady.
Collected Trot to the next letter, then ask for a downward transition back to collected collected trot.
The downward transition should come from your seat and core: stop following the collected trot rhythm and close your fingers on the reins.
Alternate collected collected trot and collected trot at every letter along the long side.
Focus on making each transition happen exactly at the letter — not 3 strides early or late.
mistakes: The transition happening too late because the rider doesn't prepare early enough.
The trot departure being abrupt and fast instead of flowing forward smoothly.
The walk transition taking too many strides — the horse leaking forward instead of responding promptly.
The rider using too much rein for the downward transition instead of the seat and core.
variations: Add halt transitions between the walk-trot transitions for three-gait work.
Progress to trot-canter transitions at the letters.
Perform the transitions on a 20-meter circle instead of the long side.
tips: Tip: Prepare your transition 3 strides before the letter. Think of it as: prepare, ask, allow.

title: Haunches-In (Travers) at the Walk
steps: Begin at the collected walk, tracking right on the long side. Your horse should be straight and balanced.
As you leave the corner, keep the shoulders on the track but bring the haunches to the inside of the arena.
Your outside (right) leg behind the girth actively pushes the haunches inward. Your inside (left) leg at the girth maintains impulsion.
The horse should be on four tracks with the bend in the direction of travel — bent right while moving right.
Maintain approximately 35 degrees of angle from the rail. The horse looks in the direction it's traveling.
Ride haunches-in for the length of the long side, then straighten before the corner.
This is more difficult than shoulder-in because the horse bends toward the direction of movement.
mistakes: Too much angle causing the horse to lose impulsion and rhythm.
The horse tilting its head instead of bending uniformly through the body.
Losing the haunches as the horse straightens itself halfway down the long side.
Confusing haunches-in with leg yield — in travers, the horse bends toward the direction of travel.
variations: Start with haunches-in at the walk to establish the feel of the movement.
Progress from shoulder-in directly to haunches-in on the long side for a seamless transition.
Use haunches-in as preparation for half-pass: the body position is identical.
tips: Tip: Haunches-in is the mirror of shoulder-in. In shoulder-in, the shoulders come in; in haunches-in, the haunches come in. The bend always follows the direction of travel in travers.

title: Canter Circle with Walk Transitions
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Begin at the working walk on the correct lead.
At the first quarter-point cone, transition to walk for 4-6 strides.
At the next quarter-point, pick up the walk again. The transition should be prompt and on the correct lead.
Continue this pattern: walk three-quarters, walk one-quarter, around the circle.
After 3 repetitions, reverse the ratio: walk three-quarters and walk only one-quarter of the circle.
The walk-to-walk transitions on a circle test your horse's responsiveness and balance.
Finish with a full circle of balanced walk, then walk and stretch.
mistakes: Picking up the wrong lead after the trot interlude.
The trot section becoming rushed as the horse anticipates the canter depart.
Losing the circle shape during transitions.
The canter becoming flat and running after multiple transitions.
variations: Use walk transitions instead of trot for a more advanced collection exercise.
Reduce to 2-3 trot strides between canter segments for sharper transitions.
Perform on a 15-meter circle to increase the balance demand.
tips: Tip: If the horse picks up the wrong lead, calmly trot and try again. Don't punish, just redirect.

title: Canter Circle with Trot Transitions at the Trot
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Begin at the working trot on the correct lead.
At the first quarter-point cone, transition to trot for 4-6 strides.
At the next quarter-point, pick up the trot again. The transition should be prompt and on the correct lead.
Continue this pattern: trot three-quarters, trot one-quarter, around the circle.
After 3 repetitions, reverse the ratio: trot three-quarters and trot only one-quarter of the circle.
The trot-to-trot transitions on a circle test your horse's responsiveness and balance.
Finish with a full circle of balanced trot, then trot and stretch.
mistakes: Picking up the wrong lead after the trot interlude.
The trot section becoming rushed as the horse anticipates the canter depart.
Losing the circle shape during transitions.
The canter becoming flat and running after multiple transitions.
variations: Use walk transitions instead of trot for a more advanced collection exercise.
Reduce to 2-3 trot strides between canter segments for sharper transitions.
Perform on a 15-meter circle to increase the balance demand.
tips: Tip: If the horse picks up the wrong lead, calmly trot and try again. Don't punish, just redirect.

title: Canter Circle with Canter Transitions
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Begin at the working canter on the correct lead.
At the first quarter-point cone, transition to canter for 4-6 strides.
At the next quarter-point, pick up the canter again. The transition should be prompt and on the correct lead.
Continue this pattern: canter three-quarters, canter one-quarter, around the circle.
After 3 repetitions, reverse the ratio: canter three-quarters and canter only one-quarter of the circle.
The canter-to-canter transitions on a circle test your horse's responsiveness and balance.
Finish with a full circle of balanced canter, then canter and stretch.
mistakes: Picking up the wrong lead after the trot interlude.

title: Canter Circle with Sitting Trot Transitions
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Begin at the working sitting trot on the correct lead.
At the first quarter-point cone, transition to sitting trot for 4-6 strides.
At the next quarter-point, pick up the sitting trot again. The transition should be prompt and on the correct lead.
Continue this pattern: sitting trot three-quarters, sitting trot one-quarter, around the circle.
After 3 repetitions, reverse the ratio: sitting trot three-quarters and sitting trot only one-quarter of the circle.
The sitting trot-to-sitting trot transitions on a circle test your horse's responsiveness and balance.
Finish with a full circle of balanced sitting trot, then sitting sitting trot and stretch.
mistakes: Picking up the wrong lead after the trot interlude.
The trot section becoming rushed as the horse anticipates the canter depart.
Losing the circle shape during transitions.
The canter becoming flat and running after multiple transitions.

Reduce to 2-3 trot strides between canter segments for sharper transitions.
Perform on a 15-meter circle to increase the balance demand.

title: Canter Circle with Collected Trot Transitions
steps: Set up a 20-meter circle. Begin at the working collected trot on the correct lead.
At the first quarter-point cone, transition to collected trot for 4-6 strides.
At the next quarter-point, pick up the collected trot again. The transition should be prompt and on the correct lead.
Continue this pattern: collected trot three-quarters, collected trot one-quarter, around the circle.
After 3 repetitions, reverse the ratio: collected trot three-quarters and collected trot only one-quarter of the circle.
The collected trot-to-collected trot transitions on a circle test your horse's responsiveness and balance.
Finish with a full circle of balanced collected trot, then collected collected trot and stretch.
mistakes: Picking up the wrong lead after the trot interlude.
The trot section becoming rushed as the horse anticipates the canter depart.
Losing the circle shape during transitions.
The canter becoming flat and running after multiple transitions.
variations: Use walk transitions instead of trot for a more advanced collection exercise.
Reduce to 2-3 trot strides between canter segments for sharper transitions.
Perform on a 15-meter circle to increase the balance demand.
tips: Tip: If the horse picks up the wrong lead, calmly trot and try again. Don't punish, just redirect.

title: Extended Trot Development at the Trot
steps: Begin with a working trot, then collect for 8-10 strides to store energy in the hindquarters.
At the beginning of the diagonal, release that stored energy: allow the horse to push powerfully from behind.
The extended trot should show maximum ground cover with each stride. The legs should reach forward, not just up.
Your posting becomes bigger and slower to match the longer stride. Let your hands follow the head motion.
Maintain the extension across the full diagonal, keeping the rhythm steady.
Before the track, collect firmly over 4-5 strides. Don't slam on the brakes.
Trot on a long rein and assess: did the horse push more from behind or just run faster?
mistakes: Confusing extension with speed — the horse should lengthen the stride, not quicken the tempo.
Falling onto the forehand because the horse is pushing flat instead of uphill.
Losing the collection at the end, arriving at the track unbalanced.
Tension in the horse's back causing short, tight steps instead of sweeping extensions.
variations: Practice half-arena extensions: lengthen for half the diagonal, collect for the other half.
Compare your extension left vs. right — most horses extend better in one direction.
Video yourself to check if the diagonal pairs of legs reach equally (true extension) or if one pair is lazy.
tips: Tip: True extension comes from collection. You cannot extend what you haven't first compressed. Always collect before you extend.

title: Counting Strides Between Letters at the Walk
steps: At the working walk, count the number of strides from one letter to the next along the long side.
Ride the long side and count: H to E might be 12 strides, for example.
Now ride the same long side and try to fit exactly 12 strides again. Same rhythm, same stride length.
Change it: try to fit 14 strides (shorter, more collected) or 10 strides (longer, more extended).
Ride back and forth, changing the stride count target each time.
This exercise teaches you to feel and control stride length, which is essential for jumping and dressage.
mistakes: Losing count because the rider stops focusing mid-way.
Changing the tempo instead of the stride length to hit the count.
Only being able to lengthen but not shorten (or vice versa).
Counting every stride of the posting trot instead of every other one (posting trot has two phases per stride).
variations: Count strides on a diagonal and adjust: lengthen or shorten to hit a target number.
Apply the counting exercise at the canter for jumping preparation.
Count strides in a combination: letter A to B = 12, letter B to C = 14 (different stride lengths).
tips: Tip: This skill directly transfers to jumping distances. If you can adjust flatwork strides on demand, you can ride any distance to a fence.

title: Counting Strides Between Letters at the Trot
steps: At the working trot, count the number of strides from one letter to the next along the long side.
Ride the long side and count: H to E might be 12 strides, for example.
Now ride the same long side and try to fit exactly 12 strides again. Same rhythm, same stride length.
Change it: try to fit 14 strides (shorter, more collected) or 10 strides (longer, more extended).
Ride back and forth, changing the stride count target each time.
This exercise teaches you to feel and control stride length, which is essential for jumping and dressage.
mistakes: Losing count because the rider stops focusing mid-way.
Changing the tempo instead of the stride length to hit the count.
Only being able to lengthen but not shorten (or vice versa).
Counting every stride of the posting trot instead of every other one (posting trot has two phases per stride).
variations: Count strides on a diagonal and adjust: lengthen or shorten to hit a target number.
Apply the counting exercise at the canter for jumping preparation.
Count strides in a combination: letter A to B = 12, letter B to C = 14 (different stride lengths).
tips: Tip: This skill directly transfers to jumping distances. If you can adjust flatwork strides on demand, you can ride any distance to a fence.
